title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
text:
As a business school student specialising in Information Systems with a strong interest in the healthcare sector, I wanted to combine both fields in a concrete data project.
This analysis explores the evolution of critical care bed capacity in French hospitals between 2013 and 2024, using open data published by the DREES (Direction de la recherche, des études, de l’évaluation et des statistiques), the French Ministry of Health’s statistical department.






NoteKey questions explored




How did critical care capacity evolve over the past decade?
What was the measurable impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on hospital capacity?
How are critical care beds distributed between public and private institutions?
Which departments concentrate the most critical care resources?



Dataset: DREES – Lits de soins critiques (2013–2024)
Tools: Python (pandas, matplotlib, seaborn)

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
text:
As a business school student specialising in Information Systems with a strong interest in the healthcare sector, I wanted to combine both fields in a concrete data project.
This analysis explores the evolution of critical care bed capacity in French hospitals between 2013 and 2024, using open data published by the DREES (Direction de la recherche, des études, de l’évaluation et des statistiques), the French Ministry of Health’s statistical department.






NoteKey questions explored




How did critical care capacity evolve over the past decade?
What was the measurable impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on hospital capacity?
How are critical care beds distributed between public and private institutions?
Which departments concentrate the most critical care resources?



Dataset: DREES – Lits de soins critiques (2013–2024)
Tools: Python (pandas, matplotlib, seaborn)

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
text:
1. Loading and Exploring the Dataset

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from IPython.display import display

df = pd.read_csv("data/lits_soins_critiques_2022.csv", sep=";", encoding="utf-8")
print("Dimensions:", df.shape)
print("\nColumns:", df.columns.tolist())
print("\nYears available:", sorted(df["annee"].astype(int).unique().tolist()))
print("\nEstablishment types:", df["statut_etab"].unique().tolist())
print("\nBed types:", df["uni"].unique().tolist())

display(df.head())

Dimensions: (15141, 5)

Columns: ['id_geo', 'statut_etab', 'annee', 'uni', 'nb_lit']

Years available: [2013, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]

Establishment types: ['1_Public', '2_Prive_BL', '3_Prive_BNL']

Bed types: ['REAADU', 'REAENF', 'USIC', 'SIUNV', 'AUTSI', 'SURVADU', 'SURVENF']

The dataset contains 5 variables: id_geo, statut_etab, annee, uni, nb_lit. No missing values — the dataset is clean and ready for analysis.

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
section: 2. National Evolution of Critical Care Beds (2013–2024)
text:
2. National Evolution of Critical Care Beds (2013–2024)

evolution = df[df["id_geo"] == "00_FR"].groupby("annee")["nb_lit"].sum().reset_index()

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=evolution, x="annee", y="nb_lit", marker="o", color="#4C72B0", linewidth=2.5)
plt.title("Total critical care beds in France (2013–2024)", fontsize=13)
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Number of beds")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Evolution of total critical care beds in France (2013–2024)










TipKey observation



The chart shows a steady increase from 2013 to 2019, followed by a sharp peak in 2020 (+6% vs 2019). This spike directly reflects the emergency measures taken during the Covid-19 pandemic, when French hospitals were required to rapidly scale up their critical care capacity. From 2021 onwards, capacity progressively returned to pre-pandemic levels.

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
section: 3. Evolution by Type of Critical Care Unit
text:
3. Evolution by Type of Critical Care Unit

types_lits = df[df["id_geo"] == "00_FR"].groupby(["annee", "uni"])["nb_lit"].sum().reset_index()
types_lits["uni"] = types_lits["uni"].replace({
    "REAADU": "Adult ICU",
    "REAENF": "Paediatric ICU",
    "USIC": "Cardiac intensive care",
    "SIUNV": "Neurovascular intensive care",
    "SURVADU": "Adult continuous monitoring",
    "SURVENF": "Paediatric continuous monitoring",
    "AUTSI": "Other intensive care"
})

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.lineplot(data=types_lits, x="annee", y="nb_lit", hue="uni", marker="o", linewidth=2)
plt.title("Critical care beds by unit type — France (2013–2024)", fontsize=13)
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Number of beds")
plt.legend(title="Unit type", bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc="upper left")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Critical care beds by unit type in France (2013–2024)










TipKey observation



The 2020 surge was driven primarily by adult ICU beds. Adult continuous monitoring beds represent the largest category overall, but show a notable drop in 2024, suggesting a structural reorganisation of critical care capacity post-pandemic.

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
text:
4. Distribution by Establishment Status (2024)

statut = df[
    (df["id_geo"] == "00_FR") & (df["annee"] == 2024)
].groupby("statut_etab")["nb_lit"].sum().reset_index()

statut["statut_etab"] = statut["statut_etab"].replace({
    "1_Public": "Public",
    "2_Prive_BL": "Private (for-profit)",
    "3_Prive_BNL": "Private (non-profit)"
})

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))
plt.pie(statut["nb_lit"], labels=statut["statut_etab"], autopct="%1.1f%%",
        colors=["#4C72B0", "#DD8452", "#55A868"], startangle=140)
plt.title("Critical care beds by establishment status (2024)", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Distribution of critical care beds by establishment status in France (2024)










TipKey observation



Public hospitals account for 70% of all critical care beds in France. This distribution is particularly relevant in the context of healthcare information systems — public and private institutions operate on different IS architectures, regulatory frameworks, and data governance models, a key challenge for interoperability in the French health system.

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
section: 5. Top 10 Departments by Critical Care Capacity (2024)
text:
5. Top 10 Departments by Critical Care Capacity (2024)

deps = df[
    (df["annee"] == 2024) &
    (~df["id_geo"].isin(["00_FR", "00_MET"]))
].groupby("id_geo")["nb_lit"].sum().reset_index()

deps = deps.sort_values("nb_lit", ascending=True).tail(10)
deps["nom"] = deps["id_geo"].str.split("_", n=1).str[1]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(data=deps, x="nb_lit", y="nom", hue="nom", palette="Blues_d", legend=False)
plt.title("Top 10 departments by critical care bed count (2024)", fontsize=13)
plt.xlabel("Number of beds")
plt.ylabel("Department")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5, axis="x")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()




Top 10 French departments by critical care bed count (2024)










TipKey observation



Paris leads by a significant margin, followed by Nord and Bouches-du-Rhône. This concentration highlights a persistent territorial inequality in access to critical care resources, a known challenge in French health policy often discussed alongside the issue of déserts médicaux.

title: Hospital Critical Care Capacity in France
text:
Summary & Reflections



Finding
Insight




+6% beds in 2020
Measurable Covid-19 emergency response


70% public sector
Public hospitals dominate critical care


Paris, Nord, BdR lead
Territorial concentration of resources


Post-2021 decline
Gradual return to structural capacity



This project allowed me to apply data manipulation and visualisation skills to a real-world healthcare dataset. Beyond the technical exercise, it reinforced my understanding of how data can inform strategic decisions in hospital management — whether around resource allocation, capacity planning, or IS design.
As someone aiming to work at the intersection of healthcare and information systems, being able to read, clean, and interpret health data is a skill I consider essential for roles in MOA, AMOA, or project management in MedTech and pharma environments.

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text: This project was completed as part of the Business Applications of AI module at Dublin City University during my Erasmus exchange. The objective was to analyse the impact of the EU AI Act on organisations and produce a 2,000-word report accompanied by a 5-minute video presentation.

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text: This project was completed as part of the Business Applications of AI module at Dublin City University during my Erasmus exchange. The objective was to analyse the impact of the EU AI Act on organisations and produce a 2,000-word report accompanied by a 5-minute video presentation.

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text:
The EU AI Act






Note



The EU AI Act, adopted in 2024, is the world’s first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence. It classifies AI systems by risk level and imposes obligations on developers and deployers accordingly.

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text:
Case Studies
Three organisations were analysed:



Organisation
Sector
AI Use Case




Philips
HealthTech
Medical imaging AI


BNP Paribas
Finance
Credit scoring & fraud detection


Otto Group
Retail
Demand forecasting & personalisation

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text:
Key Findings

High-risk AI systems in healthcare and finance face the strictest compliance requirements
Organisations must implement transparency, human oversight, and risk management frameworks
Smaller firms face disproportionate compliance costs compared to large corporations

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text:
Implications for Organisations

Audit existing AI systems and classify them by risk level
Appoint an AI compliance officer or integrate responsibility into existing governance
Invest in explainability and documentation of AI decision-making processes
Engage with regulators early to anticipate evolving requirements

title: EU AI Act — Organisational Impact Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways
This project developed my ability to analyse regulatory frameworks and assess their organisational impact — a skill directly applicable to IS project management roles in regulated sectors such as health and pharma.

title: ImpulseCap

As CEO, I co-led a multidisciplinary team of 5 to design and pitch a startup from scratch in one week.

title: ImpulseCap
text:
ImpulseCap was developed as part of the 26th Challenge Projets d’Entreprendre at IMT Business School (November 2025), a week-long intensive entrepreneurship challenge in collaboration with IMT Starter, Telecom SudParis, and ensiie.
As CEO, I co-led a multidisciplinary team of 5 to design and pitch a startup from scratch in one week.

title: ImpulseCap
text:
The Problem






Note



34% of people with disabilities in France engage in no physical activity (Source: ONAPS/FNORS), often due to a lack of adapted programmes and safety concerns.


Physical inactivity and social isolation are compounded challenges for people with disabilities. Existing fitness solutions do not account for the diversity of disabilities, leaving a significant gap in the market.

title: ImpulseCap
text:
The Solution
ImpulseCap is an adaptive fitness application that generates personalised training programmes based on the user’s disability profile and goals, validated by physiotherapists.

~100 exercises covering motor disabilities at launch
Freemium model with social premium features
Target: certification as a medical device within 24 months

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text: This project was completed as part of the Advanced Operational Modelling module at Dublin City University during my Erasmus exchange. The objective was to act as a management consultant and propose Lean-based improvements for a fictitious Irish hospital emergency department.

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text: This project was completed as part of the Advanced Operational Modelling module at Dublin City University during my Erasmus exchange. The objective was to act as a management consultant and propose Lean-based improvements for a fictitious Irish hospital emergency department.

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text:
The Problem






Note



Emergency departments across Ireland face significant pressure from increasing patient volumes, staff shortages, and inefficient processes — leading to long waiting times and patient dissatisfaction.

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text:
Methodology
Three core Lean tools were applied:

Value Stream Mapping (VSM) — to identify waste and bottlenecks in the patient journey
Kaizen — to propose continuous improvement initiatives
5S — to reorganise physical spaces and improve efficiency

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text:
Key Findings



Issue
Impact




Redundant triage steps
Increased waiting time


Poor bed management
Patient flow bottlenecks


Disorganised storage
Staff time wasted

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text:
Recommendations

Implement a fast-track pathway for low-acuity patients
Introduce real-time bed management dashboard
Apply 5S methodology to medication and equipment storage
Run regular Kaizen events with frontline staff

title: Lean Management in Emergency Departments
text:
Key Takeaways
This project strengthened my ability to apply structured problem-solving frameworks to real-world healthcare operations — directly relevant to my career goals in MOA/PMO roles in the health sector.
